symbols: staff — the shepherd's instrument identifying Amos as the herdsman called to prophesy
solitary figure in wilderness — prophetic isolation and the lonely burden of God's word
heavy robes and hood — the weight of prophetic office and sober authority
barren landscape — the spiritual desolation Amos condemns in Israel's complacent prosperity
forward stride — the urgency of prophetic mission and the unstoppable word of God
description: A black-and-white engraving depicting a solitary robed and hooded figure striding through a barren, rocky landscape. The figure holds a long staff in one hand and is draped in heavy garments. He walks with purpose across open terrain under a vast, striated sky. The landscape is empty and austere, with no other figures visible. Text beneath the image identifies the figure as Amos.
theological_description: Dore's engraving captures the prophet Amos in his solitary prophetic calling. Amos was unique among the prophets — not a professional prophet or a prophet's son, but 'a herdsman and a dresser of sycamore figs' whom the Lord took from following the flock and commanded: 'Go, prophesy to my people Israel' (Amos 7:14-15). The staff he carries identifies him as a shepherd, fulfilling this biographical detail. His solitary stride through the wilderness embodies the lonely burden of delivering God's word of judgment against a complacent and prosperous nation. Amos pronounced the famous words: 'The Lord roars from Zion and thunders from Jerusalem' (Amos 1:2), delivering oracles of judgment against the nations before turning the full force of divine wrath upon Israel itself for its social injustice: 'But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream' (Amos 5:24). Amos's call from humble origins to fearless prophetic witness prefigures the New Testament pattern in which God chooses the lowly to confound the mighty (1 Corinthians 1:27). His uncompromising message of justice for the poor and oppressed resonates through the prophetic tradition and into Jesus's own proclamation of good news to the poor (Luke 4:18).

scripture_refs.reason: Amos's self-identification as a shepherd and fig-dresser called by God is depicted in the staff-bearing, robed figure
The opening of Amos identifying him as a shepherd of Tekoa and his thunderous prophetic declaration
The famous call for justice to roll down like waters — the heart of Amos's prophetic message
God choosing the humble and lowly to confound the powerful, as in Amos the shepherd becoming a prophet to kings
Jesus's proclamation of good news to the poor continues the prophetic tradition Amos initiated
